question: Define the classes P and NP. What is the fundamental difference between them?
expectedPoints: P is the class of problems solvable by a deterministic Turing machine in polynomial time.
NP is the class of problems verifiable by a deterministic Turing machine in polynomial time.
The fundamental difference is that P problems can be solved efficiently, while for NP problems, a proposed solution can only be checked efficiently.

question: Explain the Cook-Levin theorem and its significance in computational complexity theory.
expectedPoints: The Cook-Levin theorem states that the Boolean Satisfiability Problem (SAT) is NP-complete.
It was the first problem proven to be NP-complete, meaning it is in NP and every problem in NP can be reduced to it in polynomial time.

Its significance is that it provides a foundational result, showing that if SAT is solvable in polynomial time (P=NP), then all NP problems are solvable in polynomial time. Conversely, if SAT is not in P (P≠NP), then no NP-complete problem is.

question: Describe the concept of NP-completeness. Outline the steps required to prove that a new problem, let's call it Problem X, is NP-complete.
expectedPoints: NP-completeness defines a class of the hardest problems in NP; if any NP-complete problem can be solved in polynomial time, then P=NP.
Step 1: Prove that Problem X is in NP. This means demonstrating that a proposed solution to Problem X can be verified in polynomial time.

Step 2: Prove that Problem X is NP-hard. This is done by performing a polynomial-time reduction from a known NP-complete problem (e.g., SAT, 3-SAT, Vertex Cover) to Problem X. This reduction shows that if you could solve Problem X efficiently, you could also solve all problems in NP efficiently.
